beats.prompt: Two quick retrievals from the last two lessons. Match each statement to its value.

feedback.correct: Cov carries units of X×Y (which is exactly why we divide them out); independence makes the product rule exact, so Cov = 0.
hints: One card asks what units Cov carries; the other asks what independence does to Cov (L2).
Cov multiplies an X-unit by a Y-unit; independent ⇒ the product rule is exact ⇒ the leftover vanishes.
Cov is in units of X × Y; independent ⇒ Cov = 0 (#2).

beats.prompt: Given Cov(X,Y) = 12, Var(X) = 9, Var(Y) = 25, what is the correlation ρ(X,Y)?

options: 4/5 — Cov over the product of the SDs

12 — the covariance is the correlation.note: Let's test it — that's the raw covariance, still carrying units of X×Y, and ρ must land in [−1,1], so a bare 12 can't be one. ρ = Cov/(σ_Xσ_Y).

12/34 — divide by the variances added.note: Let's test it — you divided by Var(X)+Var(Y)=34. ρ divides by the product of the SDs, √(9·25)=15, not the sum of variances.

4/5 — Cov over the product of the SDs.note: Good instinct — divide by the product of the SDs to strip the units: 12/√225 = 12/15 = 4/5 (#5).

beats.prompt: Quick reminder before we normalize — read the card, then continue.

interaction.title: The ρ denominator is √(product), not the product (and not the sum)
interaction.body: Correlation is ρ = Cov(X,Y) / (σ_X·σ_Y), where σ = √Var is the standard deviation. So the denominator is √(Var(X)·Var(Y)) — the square root of the PRODUCT of the variances. It is NOT Var(X)·Var(Y) (no root) and NOT Var(X)+Var(Y) (the sum). Dividing by the product of the SDs is what cancels the X-units and Y-units, leaving a pure number in [−1,1].

feedback.correct: Denominator = √(Var_X · Var_Y) = σ_X σ_Y — the product of the SDs.
hints: Read the card.
σ = √Var; the ρ denominator multiplies the two SDs.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Now compute it. With Cov = 12, Var(X) = 9, Var(Y) = 25, find ρ(X,Y). Enter as a fraction.

feedback.correct: ρ = 12/√(9·25) = 12/15 = 4/5 — the product of the SDs strips the units and lands inside [−1,1].
hints: Divide Cov by the PRODUCT of the SDs, not by anything you add.
σ_X = 3, σ_Y = 5, so the denominator is √225 = 15, not 9+25 = 34. (A bare 12 is outside [−1,1].)

beats.prompt: Stretch the Y-units and watch what changes. The raw cloud deforms vertically; the standardized cloud refuses to change shape. Does ρ move?

hero.structuralReadout: Stretching the units changed Cov, not ρ. ρ² = 1/2 holds.

feedback.correct: Stretching the units changed Cov, not ρ — correlation is unit-free. Here ρ² = 1/2 (ρ = 1/√2, irrational, so we grade the square).
hints: Predict first: as you stretch Y, does ρ go up, down, or stay?
The raw (X, cY) cloud stretches vertically and Cov scales by c, but the standardized cloud holds its shape.
ρ is pinned: rescaling units cannot change a unit-free score. ρ² = 1/2 throughout.

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: ρ = Cov/(σ_Xσ_Y) is generically irrational — the two-dice corr(X₁, X₁+X₂) = 1/√2 ≈ 0.707 is the canonical example. Quants grade and reason on ρ² (always rational from finite pmfs) and reserve the float ρ for a final decimal. And the converse trap bites in interviews: ρ = 0 means no LINEAR association, not independence (Y = X² with X symmetric has ρ = 0 yet Y is a deterministic function of X).
beats.prompt: You just saw it on the clouds. Now name it.

lenses.label: Definition
lenses.body: ρ(X,Y) = Cov(X,Y) / (σ_X·σ_Y): the covariance normalized by the product of the standard deviations. Always lands in [−1,1].

lenses.label: Irrational ρ → grade ρ²
lenses.body: The denominator is a square root, so ρ is usually irrational. For two dice, ρ(X₁, X₁+X₂) = 1/√2 ≈ 0.707 — irrational. We grade ρ² = 1/2 (always rational); 1/√2 is display-only, never a graded float.

lenses.label: The converse trap
lenses.body: ρ = 0 means no LINEAR co-movement — it does NOT mean independent. With X symmetric about 0 and Y = X², Cov(X,X²) = 0 and ρ = 0, yet Y is computed from X (fully dependent).

feedback.correct: ρ = Cov/(σ_Xσ_Y) strips units into [−1,1]; ρ is usually irrational (grade ρ²); and ρ = 0 ⇏ independent.
hints: Read the three lenses.
Normalize Cov by the product of the SDs; the root makes ρ usually irrational.
ρ = 0 kills only a linear trend — not dependence.

beats.prompt: Sort independence from zero-correlation. Match each statement to its correct completion.

feedback.correct: Independence is DEFINED by the joint factoring (multiply); Cov = 0 is strictly weaker and does not buy independence.
hints: Independent likelihoods do one specific thing; Cov = 0 is the weaker, one-directional claim.
Independent ⇒ joint factors ⇒ multiply ⇒ Cov = 0; but Cov = 0 only kills a LINEAR trend (e.g. X vs X²).
Independent ⇒ multiply; Cov = 0 does NOT ⇒ independent (#7).

beats.prompt: Mastery — two parts. Part A: the two-dice correlation. Part B: the converse trap.

interaction.scenario: Part A: For two fair dice, Cov(X₁, S) = 35/12, Var(X₁) = 35/12, Var(S) = 35/6, so ρ(X₁, S) = 1/√2 — irrational. Enter ρ² (the rational object we grade). Part B: X is symmetric about 0 and Y = X². Give Cov(X, X²) and ρ(X, X²).

fields.label: ρ²(X₁, S)  (we grade the square — ρ = 1/√2 itself is irrational)

feedback.correct: Part A: ρ = 1/√2 is irrational, so we grade ρ² = (35/12)² / ((35/12)(35/6)) = 1/2 — never the float 0.707. Part B: symmetry ⇒ E[X] = E[X³] = 0 ⇒ Cov(X,X²) = 0, ρ = 0, yet Y = X² is fully determined by X — zero correlation is not independence.
hints: Part A: ρ here is irrational → display-only; the gradeable object is one step away (always rational). Part B: compute Cov by symmetry, then ask whether Cov = 0 forces independence.

Part A: ρ = 1/√2 (shown for reference) → square it → ρ² = 1/2; enter ρ², not ρ — we never grade a float ρ. Part B: E[X]=E[X³]=0 ⇒ Cov(X,X²)=E[X³]−E[X]E[X²]=0, ρ=0; but X²'s tie to X is curved, so Cov=0 kills only the LINEAR part.
Part A: ρ² = 1/2 (#4). Part B: Cov = 0, ρ = 0 — yet X, X² are fully dependent (#7).

beats.prompt: The unit-free score, and the trap it sets.

feedback.correct: ρ ∈ [−1,1] is Cov normalized by the SD product — unit-free, usually irrational (grade ρ²). And uncorrelated ≠ independent. Next: three correlated variables, and how the geometry fences the third ρ.
hints: Read each recap card.
ρ strips units into [−1,1]; ρ = 0 means no linear trend, not independence.
Tap to continue.
